questions.question: How many bits long is a MAC address, and how is it typically written?
options: 48 bits, as 12 hexadecimal digits
32 bits, as 8 hexadecimal digits
64 bits, as 16 hexadecimal digits
128 bits, as 32 hexadecimal digits
questions.explanation: A MAC address is a 48-bit Layer 2 physical address written as 12 hexadecimal digits; also called the burned-in address.

questions.question: What is an unknown unicast frame?
options: A unicast frame whose destination is not in the MAC table; flooded out all other ports
A unicast frame whose destination is in the MAC table; forwarded only to that port
A broadcast frame destined for all hosts
A multicast frame sent to a group
questions.explanation: Unknown unicast: the destination MAC is not in the MAC table; the switch floods the frame out all other ports. Known unicast is forwarded only to the single port.

questions.question: What does ARP do?
options: Discovers the MAC address corresponding to a known IPv4 address
Discovers the IP address corresponding to a known MAC
Assigns IPs dynamically to hosts
Encrypts frames between hosts on a LAN
questions.explanation: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) discovers the MAC address corresponding to a known IPv4 address.

questions.question: What does CSMA/CD stand for?
options: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection
Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Decision
Channel Select Multiple Access with Collision Delay
Collision Sense Multiple Access with Carrier Detection
questions.explanation: CSMA/CD is Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection — the half-duplex wired method: listen, transmit, detect collisions, back off, retry.

questions.question: What is the minimum Ethernet frame size (excluding preamble/SFD)?
options: 64 bytes
46 bytes
128 bytes
1518 bytes
questions.explanation: Minimum frame size is 64 bytes (excluding preamble/SFD); shorter payloads are padded with 0s to reach a 46-byte minimum payload.

questions.question: What is the maximum Ethernet frame size (standard Ethernet)?
options: 1518 bytes
1500 bytes
9000 bytes
64 bytes
questions.explanation: Maximum frame size is 1518 bytes for standard Ethernet.

questions.question: What is the FCS in an Ethernet frame?
options: 4-byte trailer field containing a CRC used to detect transmission errors
2-byte type/length field identifying the encapsulated protocol
1-byte Start Frame Delimiter marking frame start
6-byte source MAC address
questions.explanation: FCS (Frame Check Sequence) is the 4-byte Ethernet trailer field; a CRC used to detect transmission errors.

questions.question: What is the OUI?
options: First 24 bits of a MAC, assigned to the device maker (Organizationally Unique Identifier)
Last 24 bits of a MAC, unique per interface
A 48-bit identifier for a subnet
An IPv6-specific Universal/Local bit
questions.explanation: OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) is the first 24 bits of a MAC, assigned to the device maker.

questions.question: What is the Cisco default aging time for dynamic MAC address entries?
options: 5 minutes of inactivity
30 seconds of inactivity
1 hour of inactivity
24 hours of inactivity
questions.explanation: Aging: dynamic MAC entries are automatically removed after 5 minutes of inactivity (Cisco default).

questions.question: Which Type/Length EtherType value indicates IPv4?
options: 0x86DD
questions.explanation: EtherType values: 0x0800 = IPv4, 0x86DD = IPv6, 0x0806 = ARP. A Type/Length value ≥1536 indicates EtherType; ≤1500 indicates length.

questions.question: What is a duplex mismatch?
options: One side full-duplex, the other half-duplex (often due to autonegotiation failure); causes collisions and poor performance
Both sides set to full-duplex but different speeds
Two interfaces running at the same speed but different VLANs
A mismatch between MAC and IP addresses
questions.explanation: Duplex mismatch: one side full-duplex, other side half-duplex (often due to autonegotiation failure); causes collisions and poor performance.

questions.question: What are giants on a Cisco interface?
options: Frames larger than the 1518-byte maximum
Frames smaller than the 64-byte minimum
Corrupted frames that failed the FCS check
Frames that arrived with a VLAN tag error
questions.explanation: Giants are frames larger than the 1518-byte maximum. Runts are frames smaller than the 64-byte minimum.

questions.question: Which command displays the MAC address table on a Cisco switch?
options: show mac address-table
show arp
show interfaces status
show running-config
questions.explanation: `show mac address-table` displays the MAC table (VLAN, MAC, type, ports).

questions.question: What is flooding in Ethernet switching?
options: Forwarding a frame out every interface except the one it arrived on
Sending a frame to a single destination MAC
Duplicating a frame onto a mirror port for analysis
Dropping frames that exceed the MTU
questions.explanation: Flooding is forwarding a frame out every interface except the one it arrived on — used for unknown unicast and broadcast frames.

questions.question: What is a collision domain?
options: Set of devices whose frames can collide with one another (one per switch port)
The group of hosts that receive the same broadcast frame
All devices in a single VLAN
The hosts inside a single IP subnet
questions.explanation: A collision domain is the set of devices whose frames can collide with one another — one per switch port on modern switches.
